subject: Physics
title: Young's Double-Slit Experiment
description: A fast breakdown of Young's Double-Slit Experiment for builders.

hook.text: In 1801, Thomas Young shone light through two slits and saw an interference pattern—proving light is a wave.

buildup.text: Newton believed light was made of particles. Young suspected it behaved like water waves.

discovery.text: Light passing through two narrow slits created alternating bright and dark bands—a classic wave interference pattern.

twist.text: This simple experiment overturned Newton's particle theory and dominated physics for a century.

climax.text: Quantum mechanics later showed light is both wave and particle. Young's experiment remains central to physics.

punchline.text: Two slits. Bright and dark bands. Light is a wave. Or is it?

quiz.question: What did Young's double-slit experiment prove about light?
options: That light behaves as a wave, creating interference patterns
That light is made of particles
That light travels faster through glass
